agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle-class constraints, and configurable stop duration. This spans multiple bounded contexts: route planning, fleet/driver management, delivery lifecycle, and customer notifications.",
    "bounded_context": "This feature touches four bounded contexts: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization, recalculation triggers, and stop-time estimates; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, vehicle classes, zone assignments, and shift lifecycle; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-transit, failed, completed), zone assignment of parcels, and parcel attributes (weight, dimensions, vehicle class required); (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery proximity windows, and customer communication preferences. Each context has its own language and must integrate via well-defined domain events, not direct coupling.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period for a driver, beginning when they clock in and ending when they clock out or complete all assigned deliveries. A shift is the lifecycle anchor for route assignment." },
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated to minimize total travel time or distance for a driver within their assigned zone during their active shift." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic partition of the service area. Deliveries and drivers are both assigned to zones. A driver operates within their assigned zone for a given shift." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been accepted into the system and is awaiting assignment to a driver's route. It exists within a specific zone and has vehicle-class requirements derived from its parcel attributes." },
      { "term": "Recalculation Trigger", "definition": "A domain event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces a new route to be computed. Three defined triggers exist: new delivery added to zone, delivery marked as failed, driver deviation from suggested route." },
      { "term": "Driver Deviation", "definition": "When a driver's actual GPS position or stop sequence diverges from the suggested route beyond a defined threshold. This is a recalculation trigger." },
      { "term": "Next-3-Stops Window", "definition": "A proximity notification boundary: when a delivery is among the next 3 stops on the driver's active route, the associated customer is notified." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of the vehicle a driver operates (e.g., bicycle, motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Determines which deliveries the driver is eligible to carry. This is a hard constraint, not a preference." },
      { "term": "Delivery Vehicle Class Requirement", "definition": "The minimum or required vehicle class for a delivery, derived from parcel dimensions, weight, or special handling needs." },
      { "term": "Stop Duration", "definition": "The estimated time a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes but is overridable per zone by the operations team. Used as an input to route time estimation." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that did not result in successful handoff to the recipient. Causes the delivery to exit the current route and triggers recalculation of remaining stops." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "When a driver starts their shift, the system MUST automatically generate an optimized route from all pending deliveries in the driver's assigned zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational automation policy. A future business decision could allow manual route creation or deferred assignment. The system would not be in a corrupt state without it.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver starts a shift with N pending deliveries in their zone, within [SLA threshold] seconds a route containing all eligible pending deliveries is created and assigned to that driver."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver MUST NEVER be assigned a delivery whose vehicle class requirement exceeds the driver's licensed vehicle class.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a safety and legal constraint. A motorcycle driver physically cannot carry freight, and assigning such a delivery could violate transport regulations, void insurance, and endanger the driver. If this rule is violated, the system is in an illegal state.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery on every driver's route, delivery.requiredVehicleClass <= driver.licensedVehicleClass. No route may ever contain a violating assignment."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a new pending delivery is added to the driver's active zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an optimization policy. The business could decide to batch recalculations or defer them. A stale route is suboptimal but not a data integrity violation.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver has an active route, when a new delivery enters their zone, a recalculation event is raised and a new route is generated that includes the new delivery."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a delivery on the active route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Operational optimization policy. A failed delivery could simply be dropped from the route without full recalculation in a simpler implementation. The requirement for full recalculation is a business preference for optimal remaining sequence.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver marks delivery X as failed, a recalculation event is raised and the resulting route excludes delivery X and re-optimizes remaining stops."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Optimization policy. The threshold for what constitutes a 'deviation' is not specified and is configurable. The system is not corrupt if a driver deviates without recalculation.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver's actual position or stop sequence diverges from the suggested route beyond the configured deviation threshold, a recalculation event is raised using the driver's current position as the new origin."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Customers MUST be notified when their delivery enters the next-3-stops window on the driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a customer experience policy. The window size (3) is a business decision that could change. Missing a notification is a service degradation, not data corruption.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a delivery moves into position 1, 2, or 3 on the active route and was previously outside that window, exactly one notification is sent to the associated customer."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop duration is 4 minutes per delivery stop. The operations team may override this value per zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a tunable operational parameter. The default and the override mechanism are both business policies.",
      "testable_assertion": "Route time calculations use the zone-specific override if one exists, otherwise 4 minutes. The operations team can set, update, or remove a zone override at any time."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "A driver MUST NOT be assigned deliveries outside their assigned zone during a shift.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone assignment is a geographic and operational integrity constraint. Assigning cross-zone deliveries breaks dispatch coordination, causes zone coverage gaps, and corrupts route optimization inputs. If a delivery needs to move zones, it must be formally reassigned.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery on a driver's route, delivery.zone == driver.activeShift.zone."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver with licensed vehicle class 'van' starts a shift in zone Z, and zone Z has 10 pending deliveries (8 requiring 'van' or below, 2 requiring 'truck')",
      "when": "The system generates the optimized route",
      "then": "The route contains exactly 8 deliveries, excludes the 2 truck-class deliveries, and the stops are in optimized order"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 7 remaining stops",
      "when": "A new pending delivery is added to the driver's zone that matches their vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, maintaining optimized order from the driver's current position"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and marks stop #3 as a failed delivery",
      "when": "The failure is recorded",
      "then": "The failed delivery is removed from the route, the remaining stops are recalculated in optimized order, and the failed delivery re-enters the 'pending' pool for reassignment or retry policy"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and physically drives past stop #2 to a location not on the route",
      "when": "The deviation is detected beyond the configured threshold",
      "then": "The route is recalculated using the driver's actual current position as the new origin, preserving all undelivered stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver's active route has stops [A, B, C, D, E] and the driver just completed stop A",
      "when": "The route advances so that B, C, D are the next 3 stops",
      "then": "Customers for B, C, and D each receive exactly one 'delivery approaching' notification. Customer for E does not receive a notification yet."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "Zone Z has a stop duration override of 6 minutes, and zone Y uses the default",
      "when": "Routes are calculated for drivers in both zones",
      "then": "Zone Z route uses 6 min/stop for time estimates; Zone Y route uses 4 min/stop"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A route is recalculated due to a new delivery being added",
      "when": "A customer who was already in the next-3-stops window drops out of it due to reordering",
      "then": "The customer is NOT sent a cancellation notification but will be re-notified when they re-enter the window (idempotent — no duplicate if already notified)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "Two drivers are active in the same zone simultaneously",
      "when": "A new pending delivery arrives in that zone",
      "then": "The delivery is assigned to exactly one driver (not duplicated), and the assignment respects vehicle class constraints and optimizes for overall zone efficiency"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Driver starts shift but zone has zero pending deliveries", "expected_behavior": "Driver is assigned an empty route and enters an idle/awaiting-deliveries state. When a delivery arrives in their zone, a route is created. The driver is NOT sent home or marked as unassigned." },
    { "scenario": "All pending deliveries in the zone exceed the driver's vehicle class", "expected_behavior": "Driver receives an empty route. System should alert dispatch/operations that zone Z has unassignable deliveries. The deliveries remain pending for a qualified driver." },
    { "scenario": "Recalculation is triggered while a previous recalculation is still in progress", "expected_behavior": "The in-progress calculation is cancelled or superseded. Only the most recent recalculation result is applied. No stale route should overwrite a fresher one. Use a version or timestamp to prevent race conditions." },
    { "scenario": "Driver is at their current stop (stop #1) when a recalculation moves a different delivery into the #1 position", "expected_behavior": "The current active stop is never changed mid-delivery. Recalculation only reorders stops AFTER the current in-progress stop." },
    { "scenario": "Customer notification sent for next-3-stops, but route recalculation moves that delivery beyond position 3", "expected_behavior": "No recall or cancellation notification is sent. The customer may experience a longer wait than expected. If delivery returns to the window later, no duplicate notification is sent." },
    { "scenario": "Driver deviates but then returns to the original route before recalculation completes", "expected_behavior": "If the driver is back on route, the recalculation should recognize this and the resulting route may be identical to the original. System should not enter a recalculation loop." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple deliveries to the same address or building", "expected_behavior": "These should be grouped as a single stop with combined stop duration. The system should treat them as co-located and not insert travel time between them." },
    { "scenario": "Operations team changes zone stop duration mid-shift while drivers are active", "expected_behavior": "The next recalculation uses the new duration. Active ETAs shown to customers should update on next recalculation. No retroactive changes to already-completed stops." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is added to the zone but the only active driver just completed their last stop", "expected_behavior": "The delivery triggers route creation for the driver if their shift is still active. If the shift has ended, the delivery stays pending for the next driver." },
    { "scenario": "Driver's shift ends with undelivered stops remaining on route", "expected_behavior": "Remaining deliveries are unassigned from the driver and returned to the pending pool. Customers in the next-3-stops window should be notified of the delay or reassignment." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, driver overtime, and failed delivery rates. A well-optimized route can reduce per-delivery cost by 15-30%. Dynamic recalculation minimizes wasted miles from failed deliveries or new pickups. Incorrect vehicle class assignments could result in regulatory fines, insurance claim denials, and damaged goods liability.",
    "operational": "Eliminates manual route planning by dispatchers, freeing operations staff for exception handling. Recalculation triggers increase system compute load — must be managed to avoid cascading recalculations across a zone. Zone-level stop duration overrides give ops teams a tuning lever for known problem areas (dense urban, gated communities, etc.).",
    "user": "Customers benefit from real-time proximity notifications, improving their availability for delivery receipt and reducing failed deliveries. Drivers benefit from automated routing, reducing cognitive load. However, frequent recalculations could frustrate drivers if their route changes unpredictably — there is a tension between optimization and route stability.",
    "risk": "PRIMARY RISK: The vehicle class constraint (BR-2) is safety-critical and likely regulatory. A single violation could have legal consequences. This must be enforced at the domain level, not just the UI. SECONDARY RISK: Route recalculation storms — if many deliveries are added rapidly or multiple drivers deviate simultaneously, the system could face high computational load. TERTIARY RISK: Customer notification accuracy — notifying too early or inaccurately erodes trust.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Receive auto-generated routes, reduced planning burden, but lose some autonomy. Frequent recalculations may feel disruptive. Vehicle class enforcement prevents unsafe assignments." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Gain delivery proximity notifications. Better ETAs. Risk of notification inaccuracy if routes change frequently after notification is sent." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gain zone-level stop duration control. Lose direct route control (automation replaces manual dispatch). Need monitoring tools for recalculation health and driver compliance." },
      { "group": "Dispatchers", "impact": "Role changes from route creator to exception handler. Need visibility into why the algorithm made specific routing decisions." },
      { "group": "Finance/Compliance", "impact": "Vehicle class enforcement is an audit requirement. Need logging of all route assignments for regulatory compliance and insurance claims." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 62,
    "reasoning": "The spec covers the happy path well but leaves several critical operational details undefined. The deviation threshold, multi-driver zone assignment strategy, recalculation throttling/debounce behavior, and what happens to failed deliveries after removal are all unspecified. Vehicle class hierarchy is mentioned but not defined. The notification idempotency requirements are implied but not stated. These gaps are resolvable but require product team clarification before implementation.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class constraint is a hard invariant (BR-2) — safety and legal",
      "Three explicit recalculation triggers are well-defined",
      "Next-3-stops notification window is clear",
      "Stop duration default and override mechanism is straightforward"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What constitutes a 'driver deviation' — distance threshold? Sequence deviation? Time deviation?",
      "Multi-driver zone assignment — how are deliveries split between concurrent drivers in the same zone?",
      "Failed delivery lifecycle — does it re-enter the pending pool immediately or follow a retry policy?",
      "Recalculation throttling — no debounce or rate-limiting strategy specified for rapid-fire triggers",
      "Vehicle class hierarchy — what classes exist and what is the ordering/compatibility matrix?",
      "Notification content and channel — push notification? SMS? Email? All three?"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift",
      "Vehicle class is a strict hierarchy (bicycle < motorcycle < van < truck < freight) where a higher class can carry lower-class deliveries",
      "Zone boundaries are predefined and stable during a shift",
      "Route optimization means minimizing total travel time, not distance or other objectives",
      "The 'next 3 stops' window is calculated after each stop completion AND after each recalculation",
      "Failed deliveries re-enter the pending pool rather than being permanently cancelled"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not define what constitutes a 'driver deviation.' This could mean: (A) GPS position is more than X meters from the expected route path, (B) driver skips a stop and goes to a different one, or (C) driver is significantly behind the expected time at a waypoint. Each interpretation produces very different recalculation behavior and system complexity.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["A: GPS geofence deviation (spatial)", "B: Stop sequence deviation (logical)", "C: Time-based deviation (temporal)", "D: Any combination of the above"],
      "recommendation": "Start with (B) stop sequence deviation as it is simplest to implement, most deterministic, and avoids GPS noise issues. Add (A) spatial deviation as a Phase 2 enhancement."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Multi-driver zone concurrency is not addressed. When two drivers are active in the same zone, how are pending deliveries partitioned? Is it first-come-first-served at shift start? Geographic sub-partitioning? Load balancing?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Customers"],
      "options": ["A: Geographic clustering — each driver gets a sub-region", "B: Load balancing — deliveries split evenly", "C: Capacity-based — factor in vehicle size, remaining shift time", "D: Single-driver-per-zone constraint"],
      "recommendation": "Option C (capacity-based) is the most domain-correct approach for logistics, but Option A (geographic clustering) is simpler and still reduces cross-zone travel. Clarify with product team."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The vehicle class hierarchy is referenced but never defined. We need the full list of vehicle classes, their compatibility rules (can a van driver deliver motorcycle-class parcels?), and how delivery vehicle class requirements are determined (weight? dimensions? hazmat? fragile?).",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Compliance", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["A: Define a simple linear hierarchy (bike < moto < van < truck < freight)", "B: Define a capability matrix (vehicle has capabilities, delivery has requirements)"],
      "recommendation": "Option B is more domain-correct — real logistics uses capability matching (refrigerated, oversized, hazmat) not just size hierarchy. But Option A may suffice for MVP if the business only cares about size."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No recalculation throttling or debounce strategy is specified. In a busy zone, 10 new deliveries arriving in 30 seconds would trigger 10 sequential recalculations. This has both performance implications and driver experience implications (route changing every 3 seconds).",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Engineering", "Operations Team"],
      "options": ["A: Debounce — wait N seconds after last trigger before recalculating", "B: Batch — recalculate on a fixed interval (e.g., every 60 seconds)", "C: Immediate — recalculate on every trigger (spec as written)"],
      "recommendation": "Option A with a 30-second debounce window. This balances optimization freshness with driver stability and system load."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
